description: Figure 12 presents a line graph illustrating the systematic reduction of Youngs modulus in low-density steels as a function of aluminum addition. The horizontal axis indicates aluminum content from 0 to 10 wt.%, and the vertical axis shows Youngs modulus ranging from 140 to 220 GPa. Two literature datasets are plotted: blue circular markers with a solid line represent values reported by Rana et al. [6, 20], declining continuously from approximately 205 GPa at 0 wt.% Al down to roughly 154 GPa at 10 wt.% Al; red triangular markers with a solid line represent data from Frommeyer et al. [18], covering the approximate composition range of 6.5–9.5 wt.% Al and decreasing from about 192 GPa to 170 GPa. Both curves demonstrate a consistent negative trend, confirming that increasing Al content progressively lowers the elastic stiffness of lightweight ferrous alloys. This figure substantiates the critical caveat that, although Al additions effectively reduce steel density, the accompanying decrease in Youngs modulus directly compromises material stiffness, posing a significant constraint for automotive structural applications.
